4.1.label: Question 4.1
4.1.question: Suggest reasons why accident and emergency treatment would not usually be covered under a PMI policy.

Reasons for Exclusion: Accident and emergency treatment would often not be covered by a PMI contract because:
it is a service that is likely to be provided by the State
it may not be feasible for private hospitals to provide fast and reliable emergency services, such as a dedicated emergency phone line and emergency ambulance services
due to the nature of the treatment needed, it would not be possible to perform the usual claims assessment and validation that is needed for other PMI claims

it would not be practical to impose conditions such as the pre‐authorisation of claims before treatment is received, in particular, the policyholder may not be capable of making decisions and contacting the insurer
patients requiring accident and emergency care may be less concerned about ensuring high‐quality accommodation and choice.

(i).question: Explain why it is not feasible to undertake full underwriting on health cash plans.

Why is full underwriting not feasible?: Cash plans are short‐term, renewable contracts, and part of their appeal is that they have relatively small premiums.  The insurer must therefore aim to keep premiums down and to recoup initial expenses quickly, as non‐renewal rates may be significant.
Full medical underwriting can make a considerable contribution to initial expenses and so would represent a considerable loading on the premiums.
This is likely to lead to reduced overall sales, and thus make it more difficult to cover fixed costs.
Cash plans are also appealing in their simplicity, however, full underwriting takes time and so delays the acceptance of a proposal.  This can act as an additional disincentive to applicants.

(ii).question: Describe each of the following conditions and explain their purpose: (a) a waiting period before a policyholder becomes eligible for benefits (b) a moratorium on claims related to pre‐existing conditions.

Policy conditions: Both measures are designed to prevent selection against the insurer, in particular when the insurer only undertakes limited underwriting at the start of the policy. [½]
(a) Waiting period before a policyholder becomes eligible for benefits
A waiting period is an initial period at the beginning of the policy, during which premiums are paid, but there is no entitlement to claim.

In the absence of full medical underwriting, a waiting period discourages people who are aware of their deteriorating health, but who have yet to receive any treatment, from buying a policy in anticipation of the need for treatment in the very near future. [½]
(b) Moratorium on claims related to pre‐existing conditions

During a pre‐specified initial period (the moratorium period), the insurer will not pay claims that arise in respect of a condition that existed (or is related to a condition that existed) at the time that the policy was taken out.
This is established at the time of claim.  If the policyholder has no further treatment for these conditions during this moratorium period, this exclusion will then be removed. [½]

(i).question: Explain how community rating could lead to cross‐subsidy between policyholders.

How community rating could lead to cross: Community rating means that individual rating factors are not used to determine premiums.

To the extent that community factors do not account for all the variation in claims between policyholders, some policyholders will pay premiums that exceed their expected claims costs plus expenses, and some will pay premiums that are less than these costs.
To this extent there will be a cross‐subsidy between policyholders.

(ii).question: Cross‐subsidy usually provides an opportunity for policyholders to select against the insurer. Explain how this might happen.

Opportunity for selection against the in: Within each community‐rating group it will still be true that premiums should depend on age, gender (if permitted by legislation) and other factors.
To the extent that policyholders are aware of these differences, there is the opportunity for selection against the insurer.
For example, younger people in good health will tend to delay buying insurance until they are older and it appears to be better value.
In addition, if other insurers in the market do not use community rating (assuming this is allowed), the problems for the insurer who does use it will be exacerbated.
Better risks, such as young people, will buy insurance from the insurers that rate individually, because they will offer lower premiums.

(iii).question: In some countries, a system of lifetime community rating has been proposed for PMI.  This system allows insurers to charge premiums that vary by age at entry, but that do not change after entry provided the policy is renewed each year. Explain how a lifetime community‐rating system avoids some of the pitfalls of a more standard community‐rating system.

Lifetime community-rating system: As described above, the greatest opportunity for selection against the insurer is the fact that individual ages are not used in the rating process.
If premiums can vary by age at entry, then the insurer can determine an appropriate level premium to cover the increasing risk over the expected policy lifetime of the insured.

In this case, each individual would have an incentive to renew because they are actually paying a level annual premium for an increasing risk, so there would be no perceived advantage in delaying the purchase of insurance.
There would still be cross‐subsidy as the result of not using other individual rating factors (eg gender), but here the cross‐subsidy tends to be smaller.

4.4.label: Question 4.4
4.4.question: Define the following terms and phrases used in private medical insurance: (a) moratorium (b) coinsurance (c) age‐at‐entry pricing (d) community rating (e) elective surgery (f) investigative surgery. [6]

(a) Moratorium: Certain PMI products, in place of formal medical underwriting at inception, do not cover medical conditions that existed during a pre‐specified period (pre‐existing conditions), usually between two and five years, ...   [½]

... but will then extend cover to these conditions if no related treatment, symptoms or advice takes place for a period of (typically) two years after first taking out the policy (or after receiving further treatment).    [½]
The policyholder is, however, on immediate risk for all other conditions. [½]
(b) Coinsurance: This is the general term given to a PMI policy condition whereby the policyholder is required to pay, at least in part, for medical expenses incurred. [½]
The policyholder and the insurer share the cost of the claim in fixed proportions (eg the policyholder pays 20% of the claim). [½]
In other contexts, coinsurance also refers to the situation where two insurers share the contract and also may refer to reinsurance on an original terms basis.
(c) Age-at-entry pricing: This phrase relates to the practice in some PMI markets of calculating premiums with due allowance for the increasing age of the policyholder over the prospective period of cover (often to age 65). [½]
Provided policyholders renew their policies each year, premiums will not increase because of the policyholder’s increasing age. [½]
The insurer usually retains the right to increase premiums subsequently to allow for medical inflation or medical inflation in excess of levels assumed in the original calculation.
(d) Community rating: Community rating most often refers to the practice of charging all policyholders or a significant subset of policyholders the same premium rate ...   [½]
... irrespective of rating factors such as age, gender and medical history. [½]
Community rating sometimes refers to the process of applying tabular rates to applicants irrespective of claims history. [½]
(e) Elective surgery: This is surgery that is not deemed necessary or even advisable, but that the patient chooses to undergo.  It is therefore deemed to be ‘elective’ and is seldom covered by standard PMI plans.  [½]
For example, the cure of an acute condition is generally covered by PMI plans but cosmetic surgery is not. [½]
(f) Investigative surgery: This is surgery that is undergone in order to advance the diagnosis, to determine the nature and extent of the complaint. [½]
It is generally covered under PMI products (but may not be so under MME products). [½]

4.5.label: Question 4.5
4.5.question: Describe the advantages and disadvantages for a private medical insurer of controlling claim costs in each of the following ways:  a co‐insurance arrangement, whereby the insurer pays 75% of each and every claim  a no claims discount (NCD) scheme with a 10% discount after one claim‐free year and 25% discount after two claim‐free years; after a claim the discount level reverts to 0%  a maximum claims payment of £10,000 in any policy year  an excess of £1,000 applied to each and every claim. [8]

Co-insurance: + There is an element of claims control because the cost of each claim is shared with the policyholder.      [½]
In particular, the policyholder is less likely to make false claims / exaggerate the size of a claim.    [½]
+ Premiums will be more competitive than for a policy without co‐insurance, resulting in higher sales. [½]
+ If the feature is novel in the market, it may result in higher sales. [½]
– The insurer will be involved in all claims, so claims administration costs will be high. [½]
– There is a no limit to the amount that the policyholder may have to pay.  This represents a risk for the policyholder.    [½]
Indeed, if the policyholder is unable to afford to contribute to very large claims, this may lead to bad publicity that may harm the insurer’s reputation.   [½]
– There is no limit to the amount that the insurer may have to pay. [½]
NCD scheme: + It will encourage the policyholder to pay for small claims, thus reducing the number of claims ...   [½]
... and keeping down claims administration costs.   [½]
– It may be viewed as immoral by the industry because it discourages people from claiming when they need treatment. [½]
– It will increase other administration costs and it is necessary to ensure that the systems can deal with the ratings imposed. [½]
+ It may encourage policyholders to renew their policies, particularly if the discount is not transferable between insurers. [½]
Maximum claims payment in any policy yea: + It will limit the insurer’s liability, resulting in lower claims overall.    [½]
This means that premiums can be reduced to more competitive levels, and this should lead to more sales.   [½]

– If the policyholder is seriously ill (eg needs an organ transplant), the insurance cover may run out before the treatment is complete.  There is then a risk of bad publicity harming the insurer’s reputation. [½]

– This would have most effect at older ages, where claims are more frequent.  The policyholder may feel that he or she has paid for many years without claiming, and so any restriction may appear to be unfair (in spite of the policy wording). [½]
– The insurer will still have to pay all claims, so the claims administration expenses will be high. [½]
+/– The overall impact is likely to be small because most cases where expensive or lengthy treatment is required will be for chronic conditions, which are excluded from most PMI policies. [½]
An excess of £1,000 on each claim: + This would act to reduce the number of claims, so reducing the cost of claims administration.   [½]
+ Overall claims costs will reduce, allowing premiums to be more competitive compared to those with ‘normal’ excess levels (eg £50). [½]
– It may look bad if the excess is seen to discourage people from seeking the treatment they need. [½]

4.6.label: Question 4.6
4.6.scenario: The following is a possible structure for the benefits under a dental or optical plan:  for all eligible claims the policyholder agrees to pay 25%, up to a maximum annual limit of £500   the insurer will pay the remaining 75%  once the policyholder reaches the maximum annual limit, the insurer will pay 100% of claims until the start of the next policy year.
4.6.question: State the advantages and disadvantages of this arrangement for the policyholder and the insurer compared with a plan that has a fixed excess level per claim. [4]

Policyholder: + Policyholders will receive a contribution to the cost of each and every claim.  In addition, their total liability has a ceiling of £500 each year.

+ The fact that there is a contribution to each claim means that there is some incentive to seek treatment when it is needed.  This will be advantageous for the long‐term optical and dental health of the policyholder.
– Some policyholders may think that the annual excess amount is rather high, as they may have to pay up to £500 each year.  They may prefer to pay a smaller fixed excess for each claim, of say £50.
– A fixed excess may be easier to understand than the proposed structure.
Insurer: + If policyholders receive some benefit each year, no matter how small, they may be inclined to value the policy more and thus be more likely to renew it each year, which will improve overall profits for the insurer.

– A major disadvantage is that there is the possibility of many small claims each year, which will substantially increase the expenses of processing claims.  (However, as each claim only involves a straightforward validation and a reimbursement, these costs should be controllable.)  A fixed excess for each claim would reduce the number of claims and the associated fixed expenses.

+ The policyholder co‐payment acts as an incentive for them to seek less expensive (or value for money) treatment, as they need to pay 25% of each claim (up to £500 pa), and so helps to keep claims costs down
+ The encouragement to seek treatment when it is necessary may also be an advantage for the insurer, as it may reduce the long‐term cost of claims.

(i).question: Explain the differences between primary, secondary and tertiary medical care. [3]

Primary, secondary and tertiary care: Primary care is the term given to such advice and treatment as provided by the patient’s first contact with a healthcare service, ...   [½]
... such as a general practitioner treating or managing an illness, or an emergency service treating a broken limb.     [½]
Such care is usually relatively cheap. [½]
Secondary care is the term given to such advice and treatment as provided by hospitals, consultants and other specialists, usually after referral by the primary carer, eg  general practitioner. [½]
Such care is usually relatively expensive, because it is provided over an extended period while a person is an in‐patient in a hospital or clinic.    [½]
The resources used in the treatment are also often expensive.   [½]

Tertiary care is specialised care, usually on referral from secondary medical care personnel, by specialists working in a centre that has personnel and facilities for special investigation and treatment, ...     [½]
... eg treatment in a specialist neurological unit.   [½]
Such care is usually relatively expensive because the resources used in the treatment are often costly, eg scanners. [½]

(ii).question: Explain why in many States, insurance companies restrict private medical insurance benefits to the provision of secondary and tertiary medical care. [4]

Why are PMI benefits restricted to secon: In many States, primary care is relatively cheap or is provided free at the point of use.  [½]
Further, there is usually very little delay in seeking primary medical care, eg it is possible to see a GP within a few days. [½]
In many States there is no significant choice (other than the one publicly available) of primary medical carers. [½]
All these factors mean that there is very little demand for primary medical care cover. [½]
From the insurer’s point of view, there are likely to be a large number of very small claims. [½]
Each of these claims will be costly to administer and the total cost of administration will increase the premiums offered out of all proportion to the benefit received. [½]
It is easier to exclude this benefit than to try to apply an excess that effectively eliminates it anyway. [½]
Secondary care often has lengthy ‘waiting lists’ if provided by the State.  The desire to avoid this delay is a major selling point for PMI plans. [½]
Secondary medical care plans allow the policyholder to choose her consultant and the quality of the accommodation facilities. [½]

Tertiary care is very expensive and often rationed because of the scarcity of specialised personnel and equipment.  Again, it is an attractive option to be able to bypass this rationing by having an insurance policy in place. [½]
